result:
Done — all three operations are implemented in `CsvRow.calr`, the project builds cleanly, and behavior is verified end-to-end (3 of 10 build cycles used).

The source turned out to be Calor, a custom §-tag DSL that compiles to C#, so I first pulled its syntax rules from the language docs in the Calor repo (notably `docs/syntax-reference/` and the agent-oriented `calor-language-skills.md`). What I added, following the fixture's existing signature/effect conventions:

- **`EscapeField`** (`f002`) — returns the field verbatim unless `NeedsQuoting` holds; otherwise doubles internal quotes via `(replace field "\"" "\"\"")` and wraps in quotes.
- **`JoinRow`** (`f003`) — StringBuilder walk over the list with `§EACH`, escaping each field and inserting commas between elements; the empty list yields the empty string.
- **`SplitRow`** (`f004`) — index-based scanner: a quoted field (opening `"`, char code 34) consumes content until the un-doubled closing quote, turning `""` into one literal quote and keeping commas/CR/LF as content; unquoted content accumulates until a comma (code 44) or end of line; the buffer is flushed at each comma and once at the end, so the empty line gives `[""]` and a trailing comma gives a final empty field. Character comparisons use `char-code` since Calor has no char literals.

Two Calor-specific wrinkles surfaced during the build cycles: an empty `§LIST` needs an explicit `§/LIST{fields}` closer, and the effects checker required `§E{mut}` on `SplitRow` because `§PUSH` counts as mutation (it stays pure per the spec — only a local list).

I verified with a throwaway console project in a temp directory (referencing the built `Src.dll`, deleted afterward — nothing added to the project): 18 edge-case assertions (empty field/list, lone quotes, embedded CR/LF, trailing comma) plus a 20,000-case seeded fuzz of `SplitRow(JoinRow(fields)) == fields` — all passed.
